channel.title: Comments for page "Quiet Days"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Quiet Days"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wondertainment is espacially screwed all of his creations no longer able to bring joy and happiness on a level far greater then any toy company would

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aw… that one made me sad. Especially the part about the GoI's.
"The members of Are We Cool Yet never did become cool."
- that one hurt +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They use the fountain of youth to slow down their aging process. They won't just magically age up if the fountain of youth disappears, they'll just go back to aging normally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"So it's really over?"

"As far as we can tell. You haven't teleported in the last six months, and with everything else that's happened we see no reason to expect you will."

"But the other worlds?"

"We have no idea, honestly. If you ask me, they're still trucking along just fine - multiverse theories aren't exactly outside the bounds of normal physics, after all. Sure hope the other Foundations aren't still dealing with their own horrors, though."

"Yeah. Honestly, as glad as I am that I won't run into polar bears or that goddamn smiling man anymore, something in me still kinda misses the jumps. Maybe I'm just scared of moving back to normal life, even with everything you've done to set me up."

"It's to be expected. Most of our former researchers aren't too happy either."

"And one more thing."

"Yes, Tommy?"

"Could you make sure I get the surgery before I leave? I don't want people asking why I don't have fangs."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’m the +1000th vote! Amazing tale :)

item.title: The Love That Binds Us
item.content:encoded: This song would be perfect for this story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is kind of heart-wrenching although I am wondering what happened to Dr. Clef and Dr. Bright

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this story on so many levels, and I think it really is the only "good ending" out there which doesn't break the fundamental truths that make the Foundation and the SCPs what they are.
By design, the system has no win condition for the Foundation or even humanity at large. The setting is part Kafkaesque and part Lovecraftion—bad things happen and nobody knows why. Everything is cryptic, malevolent and poorly understood, and ultimately mankind is at the whims of other forces and entities, just barely treading water over depths it can't possibly comprehend. Even when containment is achieved, there's almost always a catch or a zinger at the end, implying that it's only temporary, that whatever is locked in the box will get out one day, and the Foundation is only prolonging the inevitable because what else is there to do.

This tale takes all that and spins it into a "good ending." There's no escalation or a secret weapon, and by the end of it, the Foundation is just as blind and lost as it always was. And then, with no warning or rhyme or reason, everything that mattered to them just disappears. There is nothing to be learned from that. No purpose, no explanation, only sudden absence as if a cruel joke at their expense just abruptly stopped being funny.
Everyone is left alive and more or less well, but in the end the fact still is that humanity has no idea what is going on or why these things happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If all anomalies disappeared then so would the Spiral Path.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the only ending of Quiet Days that creeps me the fuck out

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So it starts all over again? I don't think I like your ending.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There goes the feels.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The old man sat quietly at the peeling wooden stand, looking out through the hot plains around him. He idly scuffed his feet against the concrete below him, which was pockmarked with dents and chips. A faded block of concrete and wood, in the middle of nowhere. He reached up to his chest, and unhooked a faded piece of plastic, the words marked on it barely readable.

SC- Fou-ati-n
Resear-r Fl-er C-llow-y

Staring at it for a moment, he pulled his arm back, and threw it out into the desert, watching it bounce away. He stood, groaning as his joints complained, and walked to the stairs down the side of the platform. He stood on dirt for the first time in 25 years. He stopped, listening carefully. Was that the faint whistle of an approaching train? Looking around, he sighed, and began to walk into the desert, slowly fading into the distance.

The train wasn't coming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I went searching for my login details again just so I could drop this an upvote. The best ending, oddly enough, is possibly the saddest of them all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And then someone rediscovered the Spiral Path…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes that would have been nice, but that version would have detracted from the article significantly/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This kinda hurt.

Dang.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Everything was gone. No matter where you looked, it seemed like a normal research society. The researcher frantically went by checking containment units, begging for this not to be the end. SCP-163 was gone, as was the craft they had found it in, back to some distant star on a planet orbiting a star in a galaxy we barely know of. 399 was similarly missing, and its room at a perfectly normal temperature.
He checked his messages. Hundreds of anomalies had been already confirmed to do nothing. 1171, 1471, 3008, 2625, 3081, 507, 5088, 2004, 3171, 983, 966, 646; more of them popped up every second, every researcher panicking and trying to sort out what was happening.
He ran by a cell where someone was testing whether 3174 was still working. Panicking to be proven wrong, he shouted a number at the top of his lungs, a number that would either prove him deadly right or confirm his worst fear. "TWENTY-FIVE TWENTY-ONE!
…
A minute passed and he began to cry. His research, his life's work, his purpose and meaning, gone in an instant. "WHY?!?!" he shouted over and over, being unable to say anything else. He needed to be comforted and not even 999 could help him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry for the necropost of 6 1/2 years, but what was the song if you remember? That youtube account was terminated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A small ficlet.

When the Quiet Days happened, people suddenly remembered that there was indeed an Antimemitics Division. or there was. It seems there are perfectly ordinary ways of forgetting things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, this article hits home. Life's pretty dull if you think about it, scps have always been escapism for me. A world with jello monster that tickles you, where I am a toaster that make people refer to me as themselves, reality warpers, strange artists, literal memers. Maybe, just maybe, its real, and maybe I'm one of them. This article just make me sad, great job. The unknown makes things interesting, and you took away the unknown.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great tale, although it seems strange everybodies org would just dissolve like that. Surely they’d want to keep a small group of people researching the anomolies and their disappearance, as well as have some people on standby in the event things go back to um… normal?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, Zyn. I forgot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
June 28, 2040

In the middle of the Bay of Bengal, a submersible was lightly drifting. Two people were inside of it.

"What's that over there?", asked one of them, who was named Miller. He was pointing to a fixed ball of light in the deep.

"Gotta be an anglerfish," answered the other man, Calvin.

"This deep? Damn."

"I'm not surprised, honestly."

The submersible descended yet deeper in the direction of what must have been an enormous underwater geological formation.

+ show block
– hide block

Ananteshesha stared at the strange object approaching her eye. She attempted to conjure her previous abilities, but found they were gone.

She stared.

Yet-

The submersible angled itself towards a massive cavern at the front of the formation.

"That can't be an anglerfish."

"Dude, shut up! I'm trying to fucking concentrate," growled Calvin.

They entered the cavern.

Ananteshesha decided, finally, to engulf the object in her maw. She pierced it with her teeth.

Floodlights filled the submersible, and Miller shrieked as a massive tooth slammed into Calvin's stomach, spraying blood across him.

Miller grabbed the controls and frantically masticulated with them, to no avail.

He only had one option left, and in his fear, he dove out of the sub, whilst accidentally kicking a highly volatile engine shard towards the center of the "cave".

Ananteshesha felt a final bursting pain in her stomach, and her long life flashed before her eyes.

And, for the first time in eternity, Ananteshesha rested.

Miller almost made it out of the strange place, but a massive jaw slammed down just as he was reaching for the exit.

Miller felt tears on his face.

But were they for himself?

THE END

It's awful, I know.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the feels man

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: OK then, so those SCPs moved to that other website's universe/multiverse then. That's what I think. Also, if an author uses their own SCP in another work (isn't that allowed or are all rights given the SCP Foundation wiki?), a clone of that object/entity appears in their multiverse. So if I write an SCP based off of something from a book I wrote or put an SCP I wrote in a game, then a clone of the object will appear in the Foundationverse in the case of the former, and my multiverse/that story's universe in the case of the latter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, no, it was just von moving to RPC

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i-i-I dont know what to tell you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bright can die. I just imagine him being all like:
"What, It's over? Fiiiinnally." *stabs himself*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The rocks finally shut the hell up.

Someone pressed the button, surprised to see the garage door open after so long.

The box was unlocked, revealing nothing but a puff of dust all it fell open, much to the disappointment of several researchers with broken fingers or lost testicles.

The quiet cacti were adopted by a researcher as a decoration in their newly opened Mexican restaurant. The houseplant was taken by a researcher and given as a gift to their angsty teenage son.

The article never was finished, though from neglect rather than procrastination.

Dr. Gerald drove home without a problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The way I think of it is that all changes to the SCP Foundation wiki are automatically and instantaneously reflected in-universe. Posting an SCP causes a new anomaly to appear, which is then contained in a Foundation facility (Recovery, containment, and tests, as well as neutralization for Neutralized SCPs, are placed in their respective points in the Foundation's timeline).

Show more...
– hide block

When an SCP is deleted, that object is retroactively erased from existence, and all mentions of it in any Tales now refer to something different that is similar to the deleted SCP. Posting Tales places their events in the Foundationverse's main timeline or alternate timelines, while deleting Tales causes them to be removed from all Foundationverse timelines. Editing articles also causes changes to whatever they are about. SCP-049's rewrite, for example, caused the in-universe SCP-049 to change (as described in the Tale Over(thinking) the Rainbow. Vandalism of articles causes the objects described to change as well. Blanking makes the object disappear completely. So this means that, in my headcanon, this Tale now refers to another anomalous star, possibly the new version of the Hateful Star (“The Star, the Hateful”).

If the wiki is temporarily down, nothing happens to the Foundationverse, but all portals to alternate dimensions are locked (the Foundationverse is put under "lockdown"). Users who join the wiki become new employees in-universe, users who quit also resign in-universe. Banned/revoked users are also discharged/fired in-universe. If the wiki permanently shuts down (which hopefully will never happen), an event similar to the end-of-the-world scenario in the Tale So it Was takes place in-universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No one needs to die in the dark- there's no more dark to die in.

+1

item.title: Spectacular.
item.content:encoded:
This tale became my all-time favorite so, dear author… Bravo! Get that +1, you deserve it!

(And, uhm, you deserve all the other 751 upvotes, obviously.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was really nice, I would’ve wanted to hear, “SCP 096 became a very anorexic human, who’s face was very disgusting, but he didn’t do anything about it, SCP 173 was now a Normal statue, whoich was figured out when O5-1 closedhis eyes in front of it”

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It becomes even sadder when read in July of 2018, since the first mentioned Skip, the Hateful Star, was deleted… hope the others don't disappear…
+1

item.content:encoded: An ending that, as good as it it to everyone, still focuses on the heartbreaking part And some music that came to me as I read the tale

item.title: (no title)

Damn, tugging straight at my heart strings

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
I love this type of ending, and someone mentioned in the discussion that if it were cyclical and there was a spark after all of this that started it again, I would enjoy that too.

Anyway, ere’s my audio for this big pile of feels:
https://youtu.be/4PPiu--FYaU

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God damn it. This is the best take on the site. It's so well-executed, and super sad. Though there is one happy ending.

451 is finally reunited.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The feels. Truly beautiful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I figure they'd be treated the same as foundation personnel.

Though it'd really suck for the dimension hopper if he was in another world when his power disappeared.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If the D-Class were killed when The Foundation closed, does that mean all human SCPs would also be killed due to knowing about the Foundation? Iris the camera lady, the Reluctant Dimension Hopper, The Invisible Woman and so on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Supreme Overlord Adidal, conqueror of a thousand galactic clusters, sobbed quietly on his throne as his court hesitantly tried to console him.

Just a few years before, he'd ruled the largest empire the cosmos had ever seen, held dominion over a dozen superclusters and fifty thousand galaxies, and bent countless species minds to his will through the power of refined memetic warfare. But now, that was all done. The anomalies that enabled the great Lox expansion into intergalactic space were little more than somewhat odd rocks, while their strongest memetics, capable of forcing most minds to follow his every whim at a glance, were reduced to weird sounding bits of static. Even the formerly eternal suffering of his once great foe Ruhar could not bring him joy, for he knew that, five thousand light years away, the anomaly based torture system had already expired.

One might even suppose that, if he'd somehow been able to detect the relativistic kinetic kill vehicles launched by his former slaves before they reduced his throneworld's surface to slag, he'd have regarded it as something as a relief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, if this really matters, this is my first forum post. :)

Anywho, this was an amazing tale that almost made me tear up. I would of imagined that it would all end by the whole world going to oblivion by an end-of-world scenario, but no, it just all ends one day. Everything that was anomalous just stops one day, leaving the foundation in sorrow.

What a beautiful tale.

im not crying, you are

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nobody let her go. She vanished.

item.title: Re: Or perhaps...
item.content:encoded: Memory wipe? Remember, the memory wipers were made from the slime of a huge snake which no longer exists so the memory wipers no longer work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kinda disagree that she would be let go, along with the other human SCPS even though they are depowered. Secrets must still be kept and their freedom would be a single bullet.

item.title: Or perhaps...
item.content:encoded: This is just what agents and researchers that don't deserve to be memory-wiped are told when they're fired/retired?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Congratulations on that +600 rating! This tale really deserves it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-231-7 gave birth to a perfectly normal baby girl. The end-of-the-world scenario did not occur, and any events indicative of this have not been found. On an unknown date, 231-7 and her child were found missing from her containment chamber, along with a note, stating 231's thanks, to the disbelief of the entire foundation staff, and that she had a daughter to take care of, hence why she escaped. Procedure 110-Montauk's details were leaked to the world by an unknown source, and the Foundation came under fire. Of course, it wouldn't matter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The whole time I was expecting it to be a ruse, some elaborate way to drop the safeguards of the whole world in order to have anomalies run wild and unopposed, some sort of awful apocalypse that the foundation blames itself for not being prepared for.

This was somehow sadder

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok, this is gonna seem weird, but I just combed this whole thread and couldn't find it. Can anyone find the post where Dmatix links the track he was listening to while writing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1. Fantastic job with this tale. Made me think about how much a non foundation world would both rule and suck at the same time

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still think that it should be a cycle. Each time, some freinds find the path, or a different Scp 1, qnd each time, the organization is made, and somehow the same people are hired, since they know this stuff. It just goes on forever. Each scp 1 is the start of a new cycle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sure it's been already stated but…. global cognition hazard anyone?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But hey…. What if… there was one left?

*Cough* Story prompt *cough cough* Make it a series or something dang it *cough cough*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…And the next day, it all came back…
Would kinda ruin the whole thing. This is quite expertly written, and it deserves every upvote possible.

item.title: Re: Quiet Days
item.content:encoded: It looks like a misunderstanding followed by the question asker being silly.
